title: Implementing MOUD: Pain Management, Opioid Use, and Addiction in Alaska
teachers: Dustin Muse, Bekah Meyer, Aaron Walbrecher
description: Part 1 of 3 with a focus on MOUD
longDescription:
When: On-Demand
Where: Online
Continuing Education: Available

This self-paced online training equips healthcare providers with comprehensive, evidence-based knowledge and skills to deliver compassionate, inclusive care for individuals with Opioid Use Disorder (OUD) while effectively managing related pain. 

The course covers opioid pharmacology, pain management principles, Medications for Opioid Use Disorder (MOUD), ethical care, stigma reduction, and coordinated recovery support through interactive lessons featuring clinical scenarios, knowledge checks, and reflective prompts. 

This training fulfills the DEA’s 8-hour opioid education requirement, providing a Certificate of Completion that explicitly addresses pain management, opioid use, and addiction. Participants earn continuing education credits and practical skills to immediately enhance clinical practice.

title: Presentation and Management of Pediatric Acute-Onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome (PANS)
description: CE Available; learn about PANDAS and PANS
longDescription:
When: On-Demand Where: Online CE Credits: May be available — see provider site Cost: Free ▶ Access the Video

Overview

Internet Enduring Material sponsored by Stanford University School of Medicine · Departments of Pediatrics, Psychiatry & Behavioral Health.

This CME activity provides a practical approach to recognizing and managing Pediatric Acute-Onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome (PANS). PANS is relatively newly understood, under-identified, and under-treated in clinical practice. Infectious and inflammatory diseases can present with severe, debilitating psychiatric and neurological symptoms — and when those connections go unrecognized, clinical treatment may be missed or delayed.

The course uses case scenarios to illustrate typical presentations, physical exam techniques for neurological soft signs, and treatment algorithms. Family education, multidisciplinary coordination, and school accommodations are also addressed.

Intended Audience

Primary Care Family Practice Pediatrics Neurology Psychiatry Emergency Medicine Adolescent Medicine Otolaryngology Allergy / Immunology Rheumatology Hospitalist Urology Sleep Medicine
Northwest PANDAS/PANS Network

A 501c3 nonprofit serving those in the Pacific Northwest with PANDAS/PANS/AE and their families — raising awareness, providing education, advocating for legislative change, and supporting local research.

Visit nwppn.org →

title: A Narrow Window of Opportunity: Diagnosis and Management of Rheumatoid Arthritis in Underserved Populations
description: Education and Resources for Primary Care Physicians
longDescription:
Earn CME from this on-demand course! 
For more information, including registration, visit the Arthritis CME website.

Overview
Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disorder that is typically characterized by pain and inflammation in the small joints. If untreated, RA may introduce complications that impact the lungs, eyes, bones, and heart. Affecting an estimated 1.3 million US adults,1, 2 RA represents the most common type of autoimmune arthritis.3 Analysis of self-reported National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) data from 2005-2018 indicates a significant difference in RA prevalence among people from different races, educational levels, and poverty-income ratio groups.1

Proactively treating RA will improve the quality of life for patients with the disorder, and many patients who experience symptoms of RA will visit their primary care clinician for treatment. The New Jersey Academy of Family Physicians (NJAFP) assembled a panel of experts to improve patient outcomes by increasing primary care clinicians’ knowledge, comprehension, and performance in managing RA, especially when treating underserved populations. This website reviews current literature and guidelines and provides recommendations for using pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic modalities to develop successful, personalized management plans for patients who report symptoms of RA.

Learning Objectives
Participants who utilize the education and resources provided on this website will be able to:

Recognize signs and symptoms suggestive of RA;
Determine when to refer patients with RA to a rheumatologist;
Develop a comfort level in prescribing disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs);
Help patients understand their disease and engage in shared decision-making;
Use motivational interviewing to recognize patients’ concerns; and
Recognize and mitigate implicit bias to build a culture of equity in their practice.

References
Xu Y, Wu Q. Prevalence trend and disparities in rheumatoid arthritis among US adults, 2005-2018. J Clin Med 2021;10:3289.
Helmick CG, Felson DT, Lawrence RC, et al. Estimates of the prevalence of arthritis and other rheumatic conditions in the United States. Part I. Arthritis Rheum 2008;58:15-25.
American College of Rheumatology. 2021. Rheumatoid Arthritis. https://rheumatology.org/patients/rheumatoid-arthritis

title: Addressing COVID-19 Vaccine Hesitancy: Pregnant Individuals & Other Vulnerable Populations
description: Addressing Vaccine Hesitancy in Vulnerable Populations
longDescription:
To sign up for this training, visit the New Jersey Hospital Association Training Page. 
This training is provided by the New Jersey Hospital Association


Overview
Though much progress has been made since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, health care workers must still manage new cases, new variants, and new information. This four-module course will address vaccine hesitancy among vulnerable populations, with an emphasis on pregnant and recently pregnant individuals, and the strategies health care professionals can implement to address these issues.

Learner Outcomes
At the conclusion of this educational presentation, the participant will be able to:

Define key terms related to COVID-19 vaccine (vaccine hesitancy, vaccine confidence, vaccine uptake).
Recognize individuals and communities who are at higher risk for COVID-19.
Identify possible reasons behind lack of vaccine confidence and lower vaccine uptake among specific high-risk communities (i.e. communities of color, pregnant individuals, individuals planning to become pregnant).
Relate factors, including those related to the COVID-19 pandemic, that put individuals at greater risk for anxiety and depression during and after pregnancy.
Describe prevention and intervention approaches to help individuals cope with anxiety and depression during and after pregnancy.
Review strategies a provider can implement to reach high risk and vulnerable communities to improve vaccine confidence.

title: Alaska Peer Specialist Training and Support
description: Earn CEU's and certifications as a Peer Support Specialist
longDescription:
For information on CEUs and to Register, visit the Peer Support Specialist website.

What is a Peer Support Professional?
Peer Support Professionals are individuals who identify themselves as in recovery: living with a mental illness diagnosis, history of trauma, and/or history of addiction. They may also be a family member of someone who meets that definition.  Peer support specialists bring a different perspective when it comes to recovery and helping others through their own journeys. Being able to empathize and empower others at various stages in recovery with personal experiences is what makes Peer Support such an important part of the recovery journey and its success.

What do Peer Support Professionals do?
  • Partner with those they serve and represent them at the table with a treatment team
  • Assist consumers in understanding the process of services, forms being used, types of questions, and updates
  • Be present for the consumer
  • Provide support & encouragement
  • Assist the consumer in Voice & Choice (teach them self-advocacy)
  • Offer perspective and hope to the consumer on behalf of the treatment team
  • Offer insight and perspective to the treatment team on behalf of the consumer
  • Share personal examples
  • Support wellness and model recovery values
  • Support harm reduction as part of the recovery process
  • Support all stages of recovery and relapse
  • Offer future oriented dialogue
  • Be accepting, understanding, non-judgmental, empowering (voice, choice, affirmation, recognition, and advocacy)
Training Details
We provide weeklong, evening and weekend training options, in-person or via Zoom. Trainings are scheduled throughout the year and throughout the state of Alaska.

The training is provided at no cost through support from the Alaska Mental Health Trust Authority and the State of Alaska Division of Behavioral Health.  However, you will need to provide your own lunch and transportation.

For More Information

Phone: 907.444.3666

Email: jgalvan@akbh.org

title: Alaskan Mobile Crisis Responder:  Essential Skills and Strategies for Challenging Conditions
teachers: Bekah Meyer, Dustin Muse, Aaron Walbrecher, Katy Wright
description: Mobile Crisis Response Training:  Online CEs Available!
longDescription:
When: On-demand (flexible to meet the needs of the varying and demanding schedules of Crisis Responders)
Where: Online and easy to access.
Who Should take this Training: This training is designed for helpers in the mental health field who are preparing to enter crisis response work, including crisis teams, mental health clinicians, healthcare providers, and community professionals in rural areas.

Continuing Education Credits Available!

Overview of Training:

The aim of this training is to support crisis responders in focusing their skill sets so they enter this work with confidence, care, and respect. By the end of these modules, crisis responders will be better able to recognize what different mental health crises look like, understand basic techniques and resources for response, gain perspective to make safe and effective decisions, and remain grounded in personal well-being to provide effective care for others in their most vulnerable moments.

Learning outcomes include:

  • Recognizing and responding to a wide range of crises, including mental health, substance use, medical, and behavioral emergencies

  • Applying rapid assessment tools and triage strategies to determine risk, safety needs, and next steps

  • Building rapport and using effective communication and de-escalation techniques in high-stress situations

  • Collaborating across systems (EMS, law enforcement, healthcare, case management) to ensure warm handoffs and coordinated care

  • Strengthening responder readiness through self-regulation, cultural awareness, and practical field tools

Why take this training?
You will leave with practical skills, confidence, and strategies to support others in their most vulnerable moments; while protecting your own well-being and strengthening your role in Alaska’s crisis response network.

description: Register for CALS Training, January 23-25, 2026

This course consists of interactive sessions in cardiac, traumatic, pediatric, obstetrical, neonatal and medical advanced life support. Course materials establish the CALS Universal Approach to critically ill or injured patients, which defines the manner by which care providers evaluate and treat emergency patients.

The CALS Course was designed to provide physicians, registered nurses, physician assistants, nurse practitioners and paramedics with the
opportunity to learn and practice infrequently used lifesaving skills. Participants are encouraged to attend in teams, and courses are limited to 24 participants per session.

title: Center for Human Development Presents: Project ECHO
description: Participate in CHD's wealth of ECHOs, crossing various topics!
longDescription:
When: See calendar for dates/times
Where: Online

For more information, including upcoming events and registration, visit the CHD Project ECHO website here.

"Moving Knowledge, Not People"
These virtual learning communities provide educators, service providers, case managers, administrators, families, and others access to expert advice from professionals throughout the state and country, building capacity in home communities to implement best practices and improve outcomes. 

Hub and spoke knowledge-sharing networks create a learning loop:
Community participants learn from specialists
Community participants learn from each other
Specialists learn from community participants as best practices emerge
 
ECHO (Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes) uses videoconferencing technology to connect a team of interdisciplinary experts with primary care providers, other health services professionals, and community members. The discussions with, and mentoring from, specialists help equip participants to support individuals and their families with health and disabilities related needs in their home communities.

description: Enhance ability to provide culturally responsive care in BH
longDescription:
When & Where: Online, self-paced, approximately 2 hours 

For more information, and to register for this event, visit UW Medicine's registration website here.

What You’ll Learn:

Participants will enhance their ability to provide culturally responsive care by understanding and applying the principles of cultural humility. 

Key topics include:

  • Behavioral health equity and its role in care delivery
  • The distinction between cultural humility and cultural competence
  • The impact of implicit and explicit bias on professional practice
  • Practical strategies for fostering inclusivity and sensitivity in behavioral health settings

What You’ll Learn:

Participants will gain practical tools for navigating ethical challenges and promoting collaborative, person-centered care. 

Key topics include:

title: Foundation of Peer Support Professionals
description: Training on peer support history, values, and lived roles
longDescription:
When: On-demand
Where: Online
Continuing Education: 1 Contact Hour

Click the Register button below to add the course to your cart, and check out in the upper right!

Welcome to the School of Social Work - Foundations of Peer Support Professionals!

Peer support is rooted in the simple yet powerful idea that people who have experienced and overcome life challenges can offer a unique form of help to others facing similar struggles. What began as informal, person-to-person encouragement has evolved into a recognized and valued professional role across recovery-oriented systems of care.

This training will provide a foundation in the history, principles, and research behind peer support, explore the critical role of lived experience. By understanding the values and evidence that guide this work, attendees will be better equipped to support peer support specialist roles and services.

title: Grief Literacy for School Social Workers: Supporting  Students Experiencing Grief and Loss
description: Improve your understanding of grief in working with students
longDescription:
When: On-Demand
Where: Online
Continuing Education: Pending for Social Work credit.

To register, click the "Register" button below to add to your cart, and then check out.

The purpose of this course is to increase the grief literacy of school social workers in Alaska and to support school social workers in providing leadership to create school communities that are responsive and supportive during a time of loss. Schools are an important place for children to receive support when they have experienced a loss, because schools are familiar, students spend a lot of time at school, and schools employ many professionals who can support grieving students (Schonfled & Quackenbush, 2021). 

This training is appropriate for social workers working in educational settings that include early intervention (birth to three) and K-12 education.

The objectives of this training are to:
  • Develop basic grief literacy knowledge and skills by exploring common myths about childhood grief and loss and defining key terms related to grief and loss.
  • Understand the impact of childhood grief and loss on students.
  • Explore the roles, knowledge, and skills of the school social worker in supporting students with grief and loss.
This training is part of the Advancing School Social Work in Alaska initiative and is funded by a grant (P116Z240135) from the U.S. Department of Education

For more information about interprofessional Continuing Education, visit the Joint Accreditation website here.

Welcome to the Healthcare Provider Burnout: Prevalence, Consequences, and Solutions program. 

As you complete the 5 on-demand modules at your own pace, you will earn CME credit while exploring how large proportions of historically resilient professionals are becoming exhausted and cynical trying to deliver patient care.  The modules outline system-level causes of burnout, its consequences, which extend well beyond the providers, and what can be done to treat and prevent burnout.  Didactic modules are interspersed with interactive exercises that allow you to apply the material to your current circumstances. 

It is recommended that you complete the program over 5 weeks.  This will allow you to work through each module one week at a time.

title: Medications for Addiction Treatment (MAT) Guide, 3rd Edition
description: New Alaska-based MAT Guide: Clear, Evidence-Based Resources
longDescription:
When: On-Demand
Where: Online

For more information, visit the SOA MAT Guide here.

Medication for Addiction Treatment (MAT) is a foundation of evidence-based addiction treatment and the first-line treatment for opioid usedisorder; significantly improving treatment retention, decreasing the risk of overdose and overdose death. MAT access can help close treatment gaps, which significantly impact Alaska's rural communities. 

To enhance MAT across Alaska, a new Medications for Addiction Treatment (MAT) Guide, 3rd Edition is now available. The guide is designed to serve a broad professional audience including prescribers, care coordinators, social workers, peer support staff, and program administrators. It’s also an accessible tool for patient education and community outreach. 

The guide provides information on: 
  • Clinical overviews of MAT options including mechanisms, access pathways, and considerations 
  • Screening, assessing, and referring individuals for MAT 
  • Best practices for integrating MAT into primary care practices 
  • Common myths and messaging guidance for patient and family communication 
  • Provider-specific FAQs and links to additional training opportunities
If you, your loved one, or your patient needs assistance with finding substance use disorder treatment, go to Findtreatment.gov.  

Did you know that MAT and naloxone together can significantly reduce the risk of overdose and save lives? Make sure you’re prepared—contact
Project  HOPE or visit iknowmine.org to get naloxone and learn more. You can also email doh.projecthope.info@alaska.gov for assistance.

title: Missing and Murdered Indigenous People Compassion Fatigue Training
description: Focused on the significant effects of compassion fatigue
longDescription:
When: On-Demand
Where: Online 
Continuing Education: 2 Credits CME via ACCME. See website for more details on CME. 

Missing and Murdered Indigenous People (MMIP) Compassion Fatigue Training

This training explores the significant effects on those who aid individuals coping with stress, trauma, or pain. Specifically, this training will illuminate the toll experienced by caregivers, including healthcare professionals and public health workers, with a focus on the Missing and Murdered Indigenous People (MMIP) epidemic and the COVID-19 pandemic. Upon completion of this training, users can acquire valuable insights and knowledge and equip themselves with tools to recognize compassion fatigue in others and within themselves.

Learning Objectives
  1. Identify and define symptoms of Compassion Fatigue
  2. Identify possible causes of Compassion Fatigue
  3. Identify and assess Compassion Fatigue symptoms in yourself and in co-workers
  4. Problem-solve solutions for recovering from or dealing with acute Compassion Fatigue
Visit the MMIP Compassion Fatigue Website for more information, and to attend the training.

title: NC AHEC Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Modules for Primary Care Clinical and Non-Clinical Staff
description: Earn CEs. Twelve Modules focusing on DEI to improve patient car
longDescription:
NC AHEC Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Modules for Primary Care Clinical and Non-clinical Staff

Modules are available on demand. CEU Credits are available!
For more information, visit the North Carolina AHEC DEI module website.

Healthcare providers are required to be competent not only in medicine but also in communication and caring for people with different disease burdens, socio-cultural realities, expectations, values, and beliefs. Be it the result of a language barrier, differences in philosophy, differences in cultural norms (and expectations), or even cultural bias, lack of diversity can lead to communication breakdown with patients. Consequently, when patients cannot fully communicate or express their needs, health inequities and health disparities occur.

Health equity means ensuring that every person in every community can get the same high-quality care regardless of individual characteristics, such as ethnicity, gender or geographic location. A number of social, economic and cultural factors significantly affect both physical and mental health. Healthcare providers require education to recognize and eliminate these disadvantages that lead to healthcare disparities through initiatives that increase diversity in leadership, improve training for cultural humility, and strengthen community partnerships.

The program will consist of 12 individual modules and will focus on implementing DEI strategies that are proven to improve patient care including:
• Articulating how DEI plays a heightened and essential role in today’s healthcare environment
• Educating clinical providers and non-clinical staff on fundamental concepts, such as cultural humility, structural racism, and other relevant content to assist providers and staff to increase knowledge and adopt equitable practices  
• Integrating DEI values and core principles into the patient care environment
• Building core competency and shared language across staff

title: New England Public Health Training Center - Introduction to HIPAA for CHWs
description: Introduction to HIPAA for CHWs - Obtain CHES/MCHES!
longDescription:
When: On-Demand
Where: Online 
Continuing Education: Sponsored by New England Public Health Training Center (NEPHTC), a designated provider of continuing education contact hours (CECH) in health education by the National Commission for Health Education Credentialing, Inc. This program is designated for Certified Health Education Specialists (CHES) and/or Master Certified Health Education Specialists (MCHES) to receive up to 1 total Category I continuing education contact hours. Maximum advanced-level continuing education contact hours are 1

To enroll in this class, click the enrollment link here!

About this course
By the nature of their role, community health workers manage sensitive information about clients and communities. Community health worker practice relies on laws and protocols to guide their decisions about how to handle client information. One of the most important laws established to protect the privacy and confidentiality of health information is commonly referred to as HIPAA, also known as Health Insurance Portability Act. Note: If working in a HIPAA covered organization this training is only an introduction and you may still be required to attend HIPAA training through your employer. Not all organizations are HIPAA covered entities.

What you'll learn
After completing this course, you will be able to...
  • Define HIPAA
  • List key components of protected health information
  • Identify at least three circumstances where CHWs apply HIPAA during their workday
  • Recognize one circumstance when you have an obligation to report HIPAA covered information

title: NWCPHP Seven Directions’ Indigenous Evaluation Toolkit Training

longDescription:
Where: Self-Paced Course via the Northwest Center for Public Health Practice online portal 

When: Thursday, October 31, 2024

For more information, including registration, visit the Northwest Center for Public Health website here.

Indigenous communities have engaged in review and reflection to understand the impact of decisions and activities on community health and wellbeing since time immemorial. The Indigenous Evaluation Toolkit for Tribal Public Health Programs encourages communities to tell their own stories and reclaim Indigenous ways of knowing as a basis for evaluation, with the goals of enhanced health and wellness through improved programs and services.

Learners in this series will:
  • Articulate/describe Indigenous evaluation approaches.
  • Propose at least one way to involve community members in program design and evaluation.
  • Propose at least one way to incorporate multiple forms of knowledge into program design and evaluation.
  • Identify how indigenous evaluation approaches can strengthen a program’s effectiveness and community impact.
  • Propose at least one way to actively advocate for indigenous evaluation approaches in Western settings.
“I hope learners will leave this training with practical tools and a deep understanding of how Indigenous evaluation approaches can be applied ethically and effectively,” said Erica Ellis, Instructional Designer.

NWCPHP encourages all individuals, teams, organizations, and communities seeking a greater understanding of Indigenous evaluation and how to advocate for its use to participate in this training.

title: P.A.C.T. Practical Approaches to Comprehensive Treatment of Pain
description: Enhance pain care skills and tackle opioid abuse with P.A.C.T.
longDescription:
To register for this training, visit the PriMed Website here.


The nation is facing competing public health issues: the need to treat a large number of Americans with acute and chronic pain versus the crisis of prescription opioid abuse. NAO has partnered with Pri-Med’s P.A.C.T. Practical Approaches to Comprehensive Treatment of Pain curriculum to provide education to AHEC’s engaged in prescribing and or treating patients with opioid analgesics. This program is intended for those involved with direct patient care, including all members of the healthcare team.

P.A.C.T. Practical Approaches to Comprehensive Treatment of Pain curriculum focuses on improving clinicians’ ability to recognize, diagnose, and classify pain; educating clinicians on the full spectrum of pain management options, including non-opioid pharmacologic interventions; and providing risk reduction strategies through integration of opioids into individualized pain management plans. Presenting clinicians with a thorough review of the FDA’s “Opioid Analgesic REMS Education Blueprint for Health Care Providers Involved in the Treatment and Monitoring of Patients with Pain” will allow clinicians to provide evidence-based pain management to their patients on a daily basis. This curriculum will also provide tools to help clinicians mitigate issues associated with opioid therapies, recognizing the signs and symptoms of opioid dependence and abuse to best manage patients’ pain and medication use, and take steps to avert the opioid addiction crisis.

This activity is supported by an independent educational grant from the Opioid Analgesic REMS Program Companies. Please see https://ce.opioidanalgesicrems.com/RpcCEUI/rems/pdf/resources/List_of_RPC_Companies.pdf for a listing of REMS Program Companies. This activity is intended to be fully compliant with the Opioid Analgesic REMS education requirements issued by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA).

By participating in the curriculum, you are opting in to allow Pri-Med to provide NAO with participation and completion of participants and results for each AHEC.

title: The Menopause Transition: Optimally Protecting Emotional Health
description: Earn CME and learn about protecting patients during menopause.
longDescription:
Overview
Menopause is a universal milestone in the life cycle of women and every woman’s experience of this phase of life is unique and influenced by a variety of factors. The transition from normal ovarian function to the near-complete loss of estrogen production comes with a range of psychological, endocrinological, and physical changes which occur over years. Though some women go through the menopausal transition asymptomatic or with few symptoms, over 85% experience the effects of estrogen fluctuation and eventual deficiency – sweating, hot flashes, insomnia, discomfort, and vaginal dryness. These symptoms can range from mild to moderate discomfort to symptoms that are severe and disabling and are all influenced by psychological, ethnic, and socio-cultural factors.

Learning Objectives
Participants who utilize the enduring education provided on this website will be able to:

Recognize that in the clinical setting, women from different ethnic backgrounds will characterize menopause and its associated symptoms differently.
Employ patient-focused, culturally relevant communication techniques when counseling patients regarding menopause and their menopausal symptoms.
Utilize a patient-centered, shared-decision making approach in the evaluation and management of menopausal symptoms, including depression.
Provide objective, up-to-date, evidence-based education regarding possible menopausal symptom treatment options along with an individualized management and follow-up plan

This course is worth CME!

For more information, including registration and CME, visit the New Jersey Academy of Family Physicians website.

longDescription:
Why Take This Training?
This training is designed to equip participants with the knowledge and tools necessary to navigate and enhance workplace communication by exploring the four primary communication styles, understanding digital communication dynamics, and applying the DISC personality model to improve interactions. Through reflection and group discussion, you will examine your communication style, gain insight into how your personality influences your approach to workplace interactions, and learn how to adapt your strategies for different scenarios. You will develop skills to foster clear and effective communication, understand the dynamics of different communication styles in team settings, and create an environment that promotes understanding and collaboration.

title: Workforce Empowerment & Engagement: Exploring Healthy Conflict
teachers: Bekah Meyer, Dustin Muse, Aaron Walbrecher, Katy Wright
description: Develop knowledge and tools for conflict resolution
longDescription:
Why Take This Training?
This training aims to equip participants with the knowledge and tools necessary to navigate and manage conflict effectively by exploring healthy conflict resolution strategies, understanding the Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Modes, and applying the DISC personality model in conflict situations. Through reflection and group discussion, you will examine your conflict style and behaviors, gain insight into how your personality influences your approach to conflict, and learn how to adapt your strategies for different scenarios. You will develop skills to foster constructive communication, understand the dynamics of conflict in team settings, and create an environment that promotes healthy and productive conflict resolution.

title: Workforce Empowerment & Engagement: Personalities and Work Styles
teachers: Bekah Meyer, Dustin Muse, Aaron Walbrecher, Katy Wright
description: Develop tools for working with diverse personalities
longDescription:
Why Take This Training?
This training aims to equip participants with the knowledge and tools necessary to understand, recognize, and navigate the diverse personalities within the workplace. Through reflection and group discussion, you will explore your thoughts and behaviors to identify the unique challenges and opportunities presented by working with colleagues who exhibit different levels of the Big 5 personality traits: openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and neuroticism. You will develop strategies for fostering collaboration, understanding diverse personality perspectives, and creating an inclusive work environment that leverages the strengths of a diverse team.

title: DHA Presents: Nursing Grand Rounds On Demand
description: Free, on-demand CE credit for Nurses
longDescription:
When: On-Demand
Where: Online
Continuing Education: 2.00 Nurses (ANCC) 2.00 Certificate of Attendance

For more information, including registration, visit the event website here.

This three-part series of lectures at a Grand Rounds event, by LTC Juanita Warman, are available for viewing on-demand. 

This is a recording of the LTC Juanita Warman Quarterly Nursing Grand Rounds at Madigan Army Medical Center. The event started as a Unit Practice Council initiative and was initially conducted as an annual conference. The decision to move from a one-day conference to quarterly grand rounds was made to meet the needs of the organization. The planning committee of the grand rounds seeks to bring speakers of various expertise to help provide a diverse educational experience.

title: Ethics in DSM Practice - March 20 2026 - Rasmuson Hall
description: Join us for these learning opportunities by Dr. Meade
longDescription:
Continuing Education: 3 Credit Hours Available. See training for more details.

Vanessa Meade, PsyD, LCSW (she/her), is an assistant professor at the University of Alaska Anchorage's School of Social Work. She is also a U.S. Army/Gulf War Veteran and a former Alaska State Trooper. Dr. Meade is a licensed clinical social worker and psychologist in Alaska. She has also worked as a clinician/therapist in rural community mental health agencies and juvenile justice.

She is a certified mindfulness teacher and facilitates mindfulness-based programs at UAA with students, faculty, and staff, as well as with military veterans and wildland firefighters through online and multi-day residential retreat programs throughout the United States. She serves on several state and national advisory committees and boards. Dr. Meade also focuses on equity and social justice in her work, including within the LGBTQ+ community.

View the CACHE Refund Policy here.

title: Coronal Polishing Course - March 2026
description: Dental professionals, don't let this opportunity slip by!
longDescription:
Welcome Dental Professionals!

We now have the March 2026 Coronal Polishing course officially built in the UAOnline system. Below are the course details:

Coronal Polishing Course:
  • Eligibility: Must have at least six months of chairside experience.
  • Course Structure: Four-day course, including two online lecture sessions and two in-person lab sessions.
  • Lecture (Virtual via Zoom): March 24 & 26, from 5:30 PM – 8:30 PM (Dates/times could be subject to change)
  • Lab (In-Person): March 28 - 29, from 8:30 AM – 2:30 PM (Dates/times could be subject to change)
  • Location: UAA Dental Clinic, 3500 Seawolf Dr, Allied Health Bldg, Ste 131, Anchorage, AK 99508.
  • Cost: $375 (includes registration). Does not cover food, lodging, parking, or transportation.
Please adhere to the following steps to ensure proper registration:
  1. Apply to UAA and become a non-degree-seeking student. If you've been a UAA student before but haven't taken a UAA course for five or more semesters, please apply as a Returning Student. If you have any difficulty applying, please contact the Admissions Office.
  2. Obtain your UA email, UAOnline login, and Student ID #.
  3. Email uaa_dentalprograms@alaska.edu your name and Student ID # so that our Program Support Specialist knows to grant you the required departmental approval to take the course.
  4. Monitor your UA email closely for a notification email indicating that you have been granted departmental approval and are then able to proceed to register for the class.
  5. Register via UAOnline to DH A590-201 titled "Coronal Polishing for Dental Pros". The easiest way to search is using the CRN which is 36918.
We require a minimum of 10 students registered in order to move forward with the course.

title: Transforming Touch - Part 1 - April 8-13, 2026
description: A Body-Based Approach to Healing Developmental Trauma
longDescription:
Where: 

For more information, including registration, visit the website here.

Deepen your trauma-informed healing practice.

Transforming Touch® is a gentle, body-based approach (therapy) that uses safe, attuned touch to help heal early developmental trauma and strengthen secure attachment. Participants will learn Dr. Terrell's 7-Point Protocol, a powerful method for supporting regulation, connection, and healing in clients with trauma-related issues.

This is a one-time, two-part training opportunity for professionals and practitioners interested in deepening their trauma-informed healing practice.

If you know someone who feels called to help others heal, this training is for them. *For questions or registration assistance, contact Kathie at kkalanickse@gmail.com.

title: ACFTA Live Sessions 2026 - May 5-7, 2026 (Remote via Zoom)
description: Learn trauma informed forensic evidence collection.
longDescription:
Each victim of violence deserves to be treated with dignity and respect, and to have evidence of the crimes committed against them preserved for future prosecution. Unfortunately, many areas of the state lack the ability to document and collect medical evidence to help protect victims of crime. Forensic evidence collection requires the necessary knowledge, skills, and abilities to do so from the onset of patient care. ACFTA offers nurses and health care professionals across the state training to ensure trauma informed care for victims of violence which will ultimately promote justice for all victims in Alaska.

C.E. Credit for Nursing: 43.60  Nursing Contact Hours approved by the Alaska Nurses Association for 20.1 hours online and 23.5 hours face to face. Alaska Nurses Association is approved as a provider of nursing continuing professional development by Montana Nurses Association, an accredited approver with distinction by the American Nurses Credentialing Center’s Commission on Accreditation.

This nursing Continuing Education (CE) activity is jointly provided by the Alaska Nurses Association and Alaska's Council on Domestic Violence & Sexual Assault in partnership with the University of Alaska College of Health.

Please review this flier and FAQ's for more information.
